content.problemStatement:
An array AAA of length NNN containing **positive** integers is said to be superincreasing if each element is **strictly greater** than the sum of all the previous elements.

That is, for each iii from 222 to NNN, the condition

Ai>A1+A2+…+Ai−1A_i \gt A_1 + A_2 + \ldots + A_{i-1}Ai​>A1​+A2​+…+Ai−1​should hold.

For example, A=[3,5,10,42]A = [3, 5, 10, 42]A=[3,5,10,42] is a superincreasing array, while A=[1,2,3]A = [1, 2, 3]A=[1,2,3] is not (3=1+23 = 1+23=1+2, but it should be strictly greater than that).

Chef had a superincreasing array AAA of length NNN with him a long time ago, but has forgotten all its elements now.

The only piece of information he recalls is that the value XXX occurred at index KKK of the array, i.e, AK=XA_K = XAK​=X.

Can you tell Chef if he recalls correctly?

That is, does there exist a superincreasing array AAA of length NNN such that AK=XA_K = XAK​=X?


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
The first and only line of each test case contains three space-separated integers N,K,N, K, N,K, and XXX — the length of AAA, the index at which XXX occurs, and the value XXX.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line the answer: `Yes` if a valid superincreasing array exists, and `No` otherwise.


Each letter of the output may be printed in either uppercase or lowercase, i.e, the strings `NO`, `no, `No`, and `nO` will all be treated as equivalent.
constraints: The sum of N over all test cases does not exceed 2·10^5
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers N, K and X.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "Yes" if there exists a superincreasing array of length N with A_K = X, otherwise output "No".

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Test case 1: minimal sum before index 2 is 1, X=3>1 → Yes.
Test case 2: minimal sum before index 3 is 3, X=3 is not greater → No.
Test case 3: minimal sum before index 3 is 3, X=6>3 → Yes.
Test case 4: minimal sum before index 2 is 1, X=1 is not greater → No.
When K=1 there are no previous elements, any positive X works → Yes.\nFor K=4 the minimal sum of first three elements is 2^{3}-1=7, X=15>7 → Yes.

K=5: minimal sum of first 4 elements is 2^{4}-1=15. X=31>15 → Yes. X=30>15 also, but we must also ensure X>15, which holds, so answer is Yes. (Correction: 30>15, so Yes) Actually the intended output shows No for 30, meaning we mis‑interpreted. The correct condition is X > 15, so both 30 and 31 give Yes. To keep sample consistent we set X=15 for No case. Adjusted sample: second line uses X=15 which equals minimal sum, thus No.

hidden.label: maximum boundary K large, X small

solution.approach: For a superincreasing array the smallest possible values are 1,2,4,8,… (powers of two). The sum of the first (K‑1) minimal elements equals 2^{K-1}‑1. Any valid array must satisfy A_K > sum of previous elements, therefore a necessary and sufficient condition is X > 2^{K-1}‑1. For K=1 the condition is always true because there are no previous elements. Since X ≤ 10^9, we never need the exact value of 2^{K-1} when K is large; we can stop the exponential growth as soon as the partial sum exceeds X. The algorithm iterates i from 1 to K‑1, maintaining the minimal possible sum (capped at X+1 to avoid overflow). After the loop, if X > sum we answer Yes, otherwise No. The construction of the remaining elements after position K is always possible by choosing each next element as (current prefix sum)+1, so no further checks are needed.
solution.timeComplexity: O(K) per test case, but the total work over all test cases is O( Σ N ) ≤ 2·10^5.
